coredata.dc:title: Construction of enzyme-MOFs composite with carbon dots: A strategy to enhance the activity and increase the growth rate of the enzyme-ZIF-8 composite

coredata.dc:description: Encapsulating enzymes in metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) enhances enzyme protection and improves the accuracy of inhibitor recognition and screening. Zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) has been widely used as a host matrix for enzyme immobilization. However, challenges such as the microporous structure and hydrophobicity of ZIF-8, along with the protonation of 2-methylimidazole, hinder the maintenance of activity and the rapid formation of composite. Herein, a new strategy to synthesize novel enzyme-MOFs composite by encapsulating carbon dots (CDs)-modified enzyme and Fe3O4 nanoparticles within ZIF-8 is presented for the first time. The contribution of CDs in enzyme-MOFs composite was investigated. Characterizations reveal that the CDs-modified enzymes compete with imidazole for Zn ions, inducing mesoporous structures that alleviate diffusion limitations. Modification of enzyme with CDs also modulates enzyme-MOFs interfacial interactions, accelerating the formation of composite. Activity evaluation shows that enzyme-MOFs composite (THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8) retains 81.76 % enzyme activity under harsh conditions and maintains 66.0 % of the initial enzyme activity after 10 reuse cycles. This synthesis strategy for the novel enzyme-MOFs composite was proven to be universal. The Km value of THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 (19.32 μM) is lower than that of THR/Fe3O4@ZIF-8, indicating that modification with CDs significantly increases the affinity of enzyme. Furthermore, THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 was effectively utilized for enzyme inhibitor recognition and screening. These results demonstrate that the proposed method is a universal approach for rapidly and controllably fabricating enzyme-ZIF-8 composite with elevated activity and exceptional stability, offering promising potential for advanced drug recognition and screening platforms.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Encapsulating enzymes in metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) enhances enzyme protection and improves the accuracy of inhibitor recognition and screening. Zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) has been widely used as a host matrix for enzyme immobilization. However, challenges such as the microporous structure and hydrophobicity of ZIF-8, along with the protonation of 2-methylimidazole, hinder the maintenance of activity and the rapid formation of composite. Herein, a new strategy to synthesize novel enzyme-MOFs composite by encapsulating carbon dots (CDs)-modified enzyme and Fe3O4 nanoparticles within ZIF-8 is presented for the first time. The contribution of CDs in enzyme-MOFs composite was investigated. Characterizations reveal that the CDs-modified enzymes compete with imidazole for Zn ions, inducing mesoporous structures that alleviate diffusion limitations. Modification of enzyme with CDs also modulates enzyme-MOFs interfacial interactions, accelerating the formation of composite. Activity evaluation shows that enzyme-MOFs composite (THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8) retains 81.76 % enzyme activity under harsh conditions and maintains 66.0 % of the initial enzyme activity after 10 reuse cycles. This synthesis strategy for the novel enzyme-MOFs composite was proven to be universal. The Km value of THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 (19.32 μM) is lower than that of THR/Fe3O4@ZIF-8, indicating that modification with CDs significantly increases the affinity of enzyme. Furthermore, THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 was effectively utilized for enzyme inhibitor recognition and screening. These results demonstrate that the proposed method is a universal approach for rapidly and controllably fabricating enzyme-ZIF-8 composite with elevated activity and exceptional stability, offering promising potential for advanced drug recognition and screening platforms.

ce:section.ce:para: Enzymes, characterized by their high catalytic activity and substrate specificity, are vital for metabolic and energy conversion processes in vivo, sustaining life activities. As targets, enzymes possess high selectivity and sensitivity. The precise screening and identification of enzyme inhibitors have facilitated the discovery of therapeutically valuable drug candidates [1,2]. However, their application in drug recognition and screening is hindered by cost, stability, and reusability issues. Composite materials not only significantly enhance the original properties of the target material, but also give it new characteristics [3]. Especially, immobilization and surface modification can enhance enzyme properties significantly, thereby improving enzyme stability, enabling reuse, preventing inactivation, and expanding their applications [4–7]. Despite progress, challenges like enzyme leaching, denaturation and low mass transfer efficiency persist, highlighting the need for superior immobilized methods and carriers to advance enzyme applications.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), composed of metal nodes and organic linkers, are featured with high specific surface area, easy functionalization, adjustable pore size, high stability, and a wide variety of species [8,9]. They exhibit significant potential in the application of immobilized enzyme [10–12]. Techniques like physical adsorption, covalent binding, cage inclusion, and in situ encapsulation, have been employed using MOFs [13–15]. Among them, in situ encapsulation is regarded as the most reliable and efficient. This method involves mixing enzymes with MOFs precursors under mild conditions, allowing simultaneous MOFs synthesis and enzyme encapsulation. The enzyme promotes MOFs crystal formation and is encapsulated within lattice structure of MOFs, preventing self-aggregation and stabilizing its spatial conformation, which in turn improves the stability of the enzyme [16]. Due to its simplicity, high enzyme loading, and enhanced stability, in situ enzyme encapsulation is the preferred strategy for MOF-based enzyme immobilization.

Zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8), a representative MOF, was first utilized for enzyme encapsulation by Lyu et al. under mild conditions, sparking significant interest in enzyme-ZIF-8 composite [17]. However, as research has advanced, limitations have emerged: (1) Enzyme encapsulation efficiency depends on surface charge and properties, as electrostatic interactions between enzymes and Zn ions influence nucleation and crystal growth, favoring enzymes with negatively charged surfaces. [18]. (2) Lower ligand concentrations slow ZIF-8 formation. (3) The enzyme activity is considerably diminished after encapsulation of ZIF-8 due to two primary factors. Firstly, the microporous structure of ZIF-8, while capable of confining enzymes within lattice defects, restricts efficient substrate/product diffusion due to its narrow pore channels [19]. Secondly, the hydrophobic and alkaline conditions introduced by 2-methylimidazole lead to a conformational change in the enzyme, thereby significantly decreasing its activity [20,21]. Therefore, reducing mass transfer resistance and modulating the interaction between the enzyme and ZIF-8 are crucial to enhancing the growth rate and improving the performance of the enzyme-ZIF-8 composite.

Increasing the pore size of MOFs is an effective way to reduce mass transfer resistance, as mesoporous and macroporous MOFs enhance diffusion rates and catalytic efficiency. For example, Sun et al. synthesized highly ordered mesoporous ZIF-8 using trimethylacetic acid (PA) as a templating agent. Subsequently, 3-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane (APTS) was used to amino-activate ZIF-8(PA) and glutaraldehyde was used as a cross-linking agent to covalently bind laccase to ZIF-8(PA), forming FL@ZIF-8(PA). The physical protection provided by ZIF-8(PA) and electrostatic interactions, along with surface functional group modifications, enhanced activity, stability, and storability. FL@ZIF-8(PA) outperformed free laccase and other reported immobilized laccase systems [22]. Shomal et al. utilized alginate to modify macroporous ZIF-8 and immobilize lipase via covalent cross-linking with glutaraldehyde. M-ZIF-8 was more thermodynamically stable than ZIF-8, and lipase immobilized on M-ZIF-8 showed the highest catalytic activity and reusability, compared with free lipase and native ZIF-8 [23]. However, these methods are limited by complex preparation processes and reliance on covalent binding or adsorption. Current in situ encapsulation studies focus on microporous ZIF-8, highlighting the need for strategies to encapsulate enzymes in multistage or macroporous ZIF-8. In addition, the electrostatic interaction between the enzyme and the positively charged Zn ions controls the formation of the nucleation center as well as further composite crystal growth [24]. These electrostatic interactions can be enhanced by chemical modification of surface amino acid residues, which in turn modulates the enzyme-MOFs interfacial interactions, regulates the growth rate of the enzyme-MOFs composite, and improves the enzymatic activity of the composite. Critically, the existing methods only address one of the two key challenges, underscoring the need for simpler, versatile strategies to overcome limitations in ZIF-8-based enzyme immobilization.

Carbon dots (CDs) derived from deep eutectic solvents (DESs) have been noted for their easy functionalization and strong fluorescence properties [25–28]. Leveraging these properties, we speculate that DESs-based CDs can enhance the surface properties of enzymes through chemical modification and impart fluorescent properties to enzyme-ZIF-8 composite. Herein, using thrombin (THR) as a model enzyme, THR modified by CDs (THR@CDs) and magnetic nanoparticles (Fe3O4 NPs) were encapsulated in MOFs for the first time using in situ encapsulation method to obtain novel enzyme-MOFs composite (THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8). The role of CDs synthesized from DESs in regulating the preparation of enzyme-MOFs composite was explored. The effects of modification of CDs and encapsulation of MOFs on the conformation and activity of THR were examined. Changes in enzyme conformation and activity were explored for free THR, THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8, and THR/CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 under harsh conditions. The reusability of THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 was also examined. The universality of this method was confirmed using cytochrome c, trypsin, and glucose oxidase. Additionally, THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 was evaluated for the identification and screening of THR inhibitor using activity screening, affinity screening and fluorescence biosensing techniques. The preparation and application of THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 are depicted in Scheme 1 .
Details of materials and characterization are provided in the Electronic Supplementary Information.

The detailed synthetic procedures of CDs, THR@CDs, Fe3O4 NPs and RhB-labelled THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 are provided in the Electronic Supplementary Information. THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 was synthesized using a facile in situ encapsulation method, adapted from a previously reported literature [29]. Specifically, 5 mg of Fe3O4 NPs and 0.5 mL of THR@CDs were dispersed in 6 mL of 2-methylimidazole (1.92 M) and vortexed for 30 s. Subsequently, 3 mL of zinc acetate (0.28 M) was added, and the mixture was vortexed for additional 30 s. The solution was stirred in a magnetic stirrer at room temperature and 150 rpm for 4 h for immobilization. After the reaction, the resulting samples were separated by magnetic separation and thoroughly washed 5 times with deionized water to remove unbound enzymes. The immobilized THR was subsequently stored at 4 °C for further use. GO@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8, Cyt c@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 and TRY@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 were prepared using the same method. Additionally, Fe3O4@ZIF-8, THR/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 and CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 were prepared according to the above procedure without adding THR@CDs.

100 μL of THR/THR@CDs solution was mixed with 100 μL of S-2238 (1 mM) and incubated at 25 °C for 15 min. The enzymatic reaction was subsequently terminated by freezing the mixture at −20 °C for 5 min. The reaction product, pNP, was analyzed using capillary electrophoresis (CE). After thawing, the reaction mixture was injected into the CE system for detection.

For the immobilized enzyme activity assay, the THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 solution was sonicated for 2 min to obtain homogeneous dispersion with a concentration of 10 mg·mL−1. Subsequently, 100 μL of the dispersed solution was mixed with 100 μL of S-2238 (1 mM) and incubated at 25 °C for 15 min. The enzymatic reaction was then terminated by using an external magnet to separate the THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 from the reaction mixture. Finally, the enzyme reaction mixture was injected into CE for detection.

The new capillary was activated by sequential rinsing steps: 1 M NaOH for 30 min, deionized water for 20 min, 0.1 M HCl for 30 min, and deionized water for 20 min, followed by drying with nitrogen for 15 min. Prior to daily use, the capillary was rinsed for 5 min each with 1 M NaOH, deionized water, and 30 mM Tris-HCl solution (pH = 7.4). Between individual runs, the capillary was rinsed for 3 min each with 1 M NaOH, deionized water, and 30 mM Tris-HCl solution (pH = 7.4). At the end of each analysis session, the capillary was thoroughly cleaned by rinsing with 1 M NaOH for 5 min and deionized water for 10 min. CE analysis conditions: capillary: uncoated fused silica capillary (75 μm i.d. × 28.5 cm, effective length 20 cm), injection mode: pressure sampling: 50 mbar×5 s, separate voltage: 20 kV, detection wavelength: 405 nm, operating temperature: 25 °C, buffer: 30 mM Tris-HCl solution (pH = 7.4).
Details of the Cyt c activity assay, GO activity assay and TRY activity assay are described in the Electronic Supplementary Information.

The stability of immobilized THR was evaluated under various conditions, including elevated temperatures (50 °C and 70 °C), different pH levels (pH = 5 and pH = 7), and exposure to trypsin (TRY, 1 mg·mL−1), urea (1 mg·mL−1), and organic solvents (DMSO, DMF, methanol, ethanol, and THF). To assess the reusability, THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 was subjected to 10 consecutive enzymatic reaction using 1 mM S-2238 as the substrate. Following each reaction, THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 was magnetically separated from the reaction mixture, and washed three times with Tris-HCl solution (30 mM, pH = 7.4) to remove the residual S-2238 and pNP. Subsequently, the relative enzyme activity was measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on kinetic and inhibition kinetic parameters
ce:section.ce:para: Details are provided in the Electronic Supplementary Information.

The inhibitory efficiency of rutin, hyperoside, quercetin, kaempferol, trigonelline, leonurine hydrochloride and N,N-dimethyl-L-proline on THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 was studied. The enzymatic reactions were conducted with each compound at a concentration of 1 mM. Subsequently, the products were analyzed, and the inhibition rates were calculated.

To study the specificity of affinity screening for THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8, agatroban and warfarin were employed as positive and negative samples, respectively. 100 μL of test mixture, containing agatroban 0.01 mM and warfarin 0.9 mM, was incubated with 900 μL of THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 for 30 min at 25 °C. Magnetic separation was subsequently performed, followed by washes with 30 mM Tris-HCl solution (pH = 7.4) to remove non-specifically bound compounds. Finally, 100 μL of eluent solvent (30 % acetonitrile) was added to denature THR and release potential ligands. The supernatant was collected and stored for further analysis. Control experiments were conducted using CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 under identical conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence biosensor detection and screening of enzyme inhibitors
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence properties of CDs, THR@CDs and THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 were explored within excitation and emission wavelength ranges of 300–400 nm and 310–600 nm, respectively. The Conformational changes in the enzyme were examined at the maximum fluorescence excitation wavelength of 285 nm. The effect of agatroban on the fluorescence intensity of THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 was evaluated by adding 300 μL of agatroban at different concentrations to 2.7 mL of THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 solution. Subsequently, the selectivity of THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 was examined using 2 μM warfarin, adenosine, heparin, benzamidine, and argatroban, respectively. F and F0 represent the fluorescence intensities of the THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 in the presence and absence of the analyte, respectively.

The morphology, size and surface functional groups of CDs were first examined. As shown in the TEM image (Fig. 1a), CDs are well-dispersed and exhibit a quasi-spherical morphology with an average particle size of 2.6 nm. The EDS spectrum (Fig. 1b) reveals that CDs are rich in C, N and O. The presence of the Cl confirms the participation of DESs in the synthesis of CDs. The Raman spectra of CDs (Fig. 1c) displays characteristic D and G bands at 1366 cm−1 and 1587 cm−1, respectively, indicating the presence of a disordered graphitic carbon structure [30]. Furthermore, the XRD pattern (Fig. 1d) shows a distinct diffraction peak at 27.8°, further corroborating the graphitic carbon structure of the CDs.

The chemical bonds and functional groups of CDs were then investigated using XPS and FT-IR spectroscopy, and the detailed information is described in the Electronic Supplementary Information. The results of XPS and FT-IR characterization indicate that the prepared CDs are riched in functional groups such as carboxyl, hydroxyl and carbonyl. The enriched carboxyl groups endow CDs with the potential for surface modification of THR through covalent bonding [31]. CDs were covalently conjugated to THR using the cross-linking agent EDC/NHS. The interaction between CDs and THR was further explored using XPS, UV–vis, FT-IR, and XRD, with detailed results discussed in the Electronic Supplementary Information. Then, the obtained THR@CDs were mixed with Fe3O4 NPs and ZIF-8 precursor solution to obtain the composite in situ encapsulation of composite. FT-IR spectra confirms that composites are successfully prepared.

Subsequently, the crystal morphology of Fe3O4@ZIF-8, both before and after encapsulation of THR@CDs, was examined using SEM. As shown in Fig. 2a and b, Fe3O4@ZIF-8 retains the typical rhombic dodecahedron structure of ZIF-8 after the encapsulation of THR@CDs. During the crystallisation process of the MOFs, the incorporation of THR@CDs led to a modest reduction in the crystal size of the MOFs, suggesting that THR@CDs might have influenced crystal growth without altering the overall shape of ZIF-8 [32]. The EDS spectrum (Fig. 2c) confirms the successful preparation of the composite, with the presence of Zn, Fe, Cl and S. The elements Cl and Fe are characteristic of CDs and Fe3O4, respectively. Based on the percentage of Cl and Fe elements in ZIF-8, Fe3O4, and THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8, the ratios of MOFs, Fe3O4, and THR@CDs in the composite were calculated to be 36.39 %, 2.75 %, and 60.86 %, respectively. Fig. 2d demonstrates that although the saturation magnetic field strength of the synthesized THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 is lower compared to Fe3O4 NPs, THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 can still be efficiently separated from the reaction mixture within 1 min with the help of an external magnetic field and simply dispersed back into the reaction system by oscillation in the absence of a magnetic field, which facilitates the reuse of enzyme. The successful encapsulation of THR in ZIF-8 was further confirmed by fluorescence inverted microscopy, as shown in Fig. S4. RhB-labelled THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 was clearly observed, providing visual evidence of the successful encapsulation of THR in ZIF-8.

XPS analysis was utilized to investigate the changes in elemental composition and chemical states before and after the encapsulation of THR@CDs with Fe3O4@ZIF-8. As indicated in Table 1 , the atomic ratio of Zn:N:O in Fe3O4@ZIF-8 is 1.0:3.16:0.73, while in THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8, the ratio of Zn:O rises to 1.0:1.0. Furthermore, as illustrated in Fig. 2e, the binding energy of Zn2p3/2 in Fe3O4@ZIF-8 is observed at 1021.15 eV. In contrast, the Zn2p3/2 peak in THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 shifts to a higher binding energy of 1021.33 eV. This migration suggests the substitution of Zn − O bonds for Zn − N bonds within the THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8, highlighting a coordination competition process between Zn − N and Zn − O during the formation of the complex [33].

The coordination competition process often induces structural defects in MOFs, which can significantly impact their porosity and surface properties. The pore size and specific surface area of Fe3O4@ZIF-8 before and after encapsulation of THR@CDs were investigated to examine the effects. As shown in Fig. S5, both THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 and Fe3O4@ZIF-8 exhibit microporous structures at relatively low pressures (P/P0). However, THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 displays a type IV hysteresis loop in the range of P/P0 = 0.4–0.8, indicating the presence of additional mesopores, as confirmed by the pore size distribution results [19]. In contrast, there are no mesopores inside THR/Fe3O4@ZIF-8, suggesting that CDs may be the key factor for the formation of mesopores. To further validate this hypothesis, CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 was synthesized by directly encapsulating CDs within ZIF-8. The results of pore size distribution show that similar mesopores are also formed in CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8. In summary, THR@CDs induce structural defects in ZIF-8 through strong coordination interactions between Zn and O, leading to the collapse of THR/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 and the formation of mesopores. The mesoporous enzyme-MOF composite formed by coordination competition exhibits reduced mass transfer resistance during catalysis, thereby enhancing the diffusion of substrates and products. In addition, compared to Fe3O4@ZIF-8, which has a specific surface area of 1218.4 m2·g−1, the specific surface area of THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 is reduced to 666.3 m2·g−1. Additionally, the pore size of THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 is smaller than that of CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8. These changes indicate that the pores of ZIF-8 are filled with THR, further supporting the hypothesis that encapsulating THR@CDs creates structural defects in Fe3O4@ZIF-8. In fact, this methodology offers significant advantages, although various works have been reported on the synthesis of MOFs incorporating Fe3O4 and CDs [34,35]. The in situ encapsulation method was used to prepare the composites, which is a straightforward and efficient synthesis approach. Notably, THR@CDs introduce defects into the MOFs through competitive coordination, resulting in the formation of mesoporous structures without the need for additional templates or complex procedures. In conclusion, the proposed synthesis method is simpler and more efficient compared to existing approaches for synthesizing mesoporous MOFs with Fe3O4 and CDs.

THR is susceptible to inactivation under environmental stressors, significantly limiting its practical applications. To address this, the conformational and activity changes of free THR and immobilized THR (THR/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 and THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8) were analyzed under harsh conditions through a series of comparative experiments. The tolerance of free THR, THR/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 and THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 was firstly explored under high temperatures and acid-base conditions. As illustrated in Fig. 3a, when the incubation temperature reaches 70 °C and the pH of solution is 5, THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 maintains 62.45 % and 76.50 % of its original enzyme activities, respectively. In contrast, THR/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 retains only 45.41 % and 62.38 % of its initial activity, while the free THR enzyme activity is only 8.45 % and 45.61 %, respectively. These results demonstrate that the ZIF-8 framework provides buffering and protective effects, significantly enhancing the stability of THR under extreme temperature and pH conditions. When THR/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 and THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 are incubated in urea solution for 1 h, the enzyme activities retain 60.22 % and 72.68 %, respectively, which are significantly higher than the relative residual activities of free THR (6.55 %). This enhanced stability is attributed to the rigid framework structure of ZIF-8, which confines THR within a restricted domain, effectively preventing the unfolding of its peptide chains. The conformational changes of THR under these conditions were further investigated through fluorescence spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 3b and c, the fluorescence spectra of free THR notably exhibits a notable red-shift after heating and urea treatment, indicating structural unfolding [36]. In contrast, the fluorescence spectra of THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 does not change significantly, demonstrating the exceptional protective capability of the ZIF-8 framework for the encapsulated THR. After incubating free THR and immobilized THR in TRY solution for 1 h, THR/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 and THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 retain 49.78 % and 65.77 % of their initial activities, respectively. This is mainly attributed to the size-screening effect of MOFs, which prevents macromolecules from accessing and degrading the encapsulated THR within ZIF-8.

Additionally, the effect of various organic solvents on the enzyme activities of free THR, THR/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 and THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 was investigated. Organic solvents, upon contacting enzymes, can interact with enzymes by forming hydrogen bonds with hydrogen bond donors or acceptors within the enzyme structure. Such interactions might disrupt or alter the native intra-enzyme hydrogen bonding network, potentially leading to structural changes that affect the catalytic activity or even lead to enzyme inactivation. As demonstrated in Fig. 3d, after incubating free THR, THR/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 and THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 in organic solvents for 1 h, the enzyme activity of free THR is nearly lost, whereas THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 maintains substantial relative enzyme activity. Although enzyme-MOFs composite typically relies on the pore-domain-limiting effect of MOFs to sustain high stability, small molecule organic solvents can penetrate into the pores of MOFs, inevitably altering the enzyme structure. The hydrophilicity of the material plays a crucial role in mediating solvent-enzyme interactions. Therefore, the observed enhancement in stability for THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 may be attributed to the hydrophilic properties of the immobilized THR composites, which mitigate the adverse effects of organic solvents on enzyme structure and activity.

The hydrophilicity of THR/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 and THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 was assessed by measuring the contact angles between sessile droplets and coated slides. As depicted in Fig. 4a and b, the contact angles of THR/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 and THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 are 68.54° and 36.72°, respectively, indicating that THR/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 exhibits relatively high hydrophobicity, whereas THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 demonstrates significantly enhanced hydrophilicity. The improved hydrophilicity of THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 is attributed to the presence of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the CDs, which increase the overall hydrophilicity of the composite. The introduction of CDs into the enzyme-MOF composite not only enhances its hydrophilic properties but also promotes interactions with water molecules. This interaction facilitates the formation of a protective “water shell” around the enzyme, minimizing direct contact between organic solvents and THR. As a result, the structural integrity and catalytic activity of THR are better preserved in the presence of organic solvents.

Fluorescence spectroscopy was employed to monitor changes in the internal microenvironment of THR, and to investigate the effects of CDs modification as well as ZIF-8 encapsulation on THR conformation. As depicted in Fig. 4c, the maximum fluorescence emission wavelength of free THR is observed at 330 nm. After modification with CDs, the fluorescence emission wavelength is significantly blue-shifted to 326 nm, indicating a conformational change in THR. However, upon encapsulation of THR@CDs with ZIF-8, the fluorescence emission wavelength red-shifts back to 330 nm, indicating the conformational recovery of THR. The fluorescence emission wavelength of THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 more closely resembles that of native THR compared to THR/Fe3O4@ZIF-8. In addition, after incubating the THR@CDs solution with 2 mM KCl for 5 min, it is observed that KCl can partially neutralize the charge and reverts THR to its initial conformation. Thus, the conformation of THR might undergoes a two-stage transition during ZIF-8 encapsulation of THR@CDs. Initially, the electrostatic interaction between CDs and THR alters the conformation of THR in solution. Subsequently, the addition of positively charged Zn ions mitigates the repulsive force from CDs, returning THR to its normal conformation. The activity of THR@CDs is 48.36 % of that of the natural THR enzyme. After the encapsulation of THR@CDs with ZIF-8, the enzyme activity of THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 is restored to 82.79 % of that of the natural THR enzyme. The results demonstrate that the conformational change of THR after modification by CDs result in a significant decrease in its activity, but the conformation and activity of THR effectively restores after the encapsulation of ZIF-8. In contrast, due to the effect of the ZIF-8 framework on THR conformation and a lower substrate diffusion rate compared to THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8, THR/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 exhibits reduced enzyme activity, measuring 54.26 % of the natural THR enzyme activity.

Poor reusability remains a significant challenge for enzyme-MOFs composite. The retention of enzyme activity of THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 after multiple reuse was further investigated. As illustrated in Fig. 4d, THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 maintains 66.0 % of its initial enzyme activity after 10 cycles of reuse. In contrast, free THR can only be used once and cannot be recycled, highlighting the superior reusability of the magnetic carrier-immobilized THR. The domain-limiting effect of the ZIF-8 framework, along with the electrostatic and coordination interactions between THR@CDs and ZIF-8, provides a powerful guarantee for the stability of THR on the carrier, resulting in good reusability of THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the universality of immobilized enzyme method
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the universality of the proposed immobilization strategy, the same method used for THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 was employed to immobilize other enzymes, including Cyt c and TRY with positive surface charge and GO with negative surface charge. As shown in Fig. 5a, the zeta potential value of the prepared CDs is −15.2 mV, and this negative charge further increases further upon covalent binding to THR, resulting in a zeta potential of −8.0 mV. The zeta potential of Fe3O4 NPs is stable at −7.64 mV, which may facilitate the aggregation of Zn ions. Whereas, after encapsulation, the negative charge of the composite reduces, owing to the balancing effect of Zn ions on the negative charges. Table 2 indicates that enzymes with low isoelectric points, such as THR and GO, exhibit a negative charge during the reaction, facilitating the formation of ZIF-8. Conversely, enzymes with higher isoelectric points, such as Cyt c and TRY, has a positive charge, which hinders Zn ion aggregation and impedes ZIF-8 formation. This suggests that the modulation of electrostatic interactions through CDs-modified enzymes affects the formation of enzyme-MOFs composite in the in situ encapsulation method.

Subsequently, the crystal structure of each composite was examined using XRD. As depicted in Fig. 5b, the XRD patterns of Fe3O4@ZIF-8, enzyme/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 and enzyme@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 are similar and all of them have the typical crystal structure of ZIF-8, indicating that the enzyme immobilization does not alter the crystal structure of ZIF-8. However, the crystal strength of enzyme@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 is notably lower compared to that of enzyme/Fe3O4@ZIF-8. This reduction in crystal strength may be attributed to the formation of mesopores resulting from the introduction of defects into framework collapse of composites [37,38].

The surface charge and chemical properties of enzymes influence the formation of nucleation centers of MOFs and the subsequent growth of composite. Therefore, the impact of introducing enzyme@CDs on the growth rate of enzyme-MOFs composite was investigated. Fig. S6a and S6b indicate that in the reaction system of Cyt c@CDs-MOFs and TRY@CDs-MOFs, the reaction solution changes from transparent to opaque within 4 h, with precipitates forming. Whereas, the reaction solutions of Cyt c-MOFs and TRY-MOFs remained transparent after 4 h, with no ZIF-8 formation. It indicates that CDs-modified enzymes alter the formation dynamics of enzyme-MOF composites. Fig. S6c demonstrates that both THR and GO enhance the growth rate of ZIF-8 compared to the synthesis rate of pure ZIF-8. Notably, this enhancement effect is more pronounced when the enzymes are modified with CDs. Further investigations into the growth rate of composites were conducted by monitoring the transmittance of the solution via UV–vis spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 5c, THR, enriched with negatively charged Zn ions, accelerates the ZIF-8 growth rate, driving the nucleation and encapsulation processes. Furthermore, the negative charge of THR increases following CDs modification, further promoting the in situ growth rate of the composite.

In addition, the thermal stability of free enzymes, enzyme/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 and enzyme@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 were compared to further validate the universality of the synthesis strategy. As depicted in Fig. 5d, THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8, Cyt c@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8, GO@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 and Try@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 demonstrate enhanced thermal stability. Compared with THR/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 and GO/Fe3O4@ZIF-8, THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 and GO@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 exhibit higher temperature tolerance. In conclusion, this immobilized enzyme strategy is a versatile and efficient method for enzyme.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the enzymatic reaction kinetics, inhibition kinetics and inhibitor screening
ce:section.ce:para: Compared to free THR, the Km values of immobilized THR (THR/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 and THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8) are significantly lower (Fig. 6a). This reduction in Km values suggests that the encapsulation of enzyme with MOFs enhances its affinity for the substrate. This enhanced affinity is attributed not only to the more favorable microenvironment provided by immobilization but also to the potential enrichment effect of ZIF-8 on the substrate [39]. The catalytic activity of THR/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 is lower than that of THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8, primarily due to the restricted substrate diffusion and conformational changes induced by the ZIF-8 framework. The slightly larger pore structure of THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 facilitates substrate and product diffusion, resulting in reducing mass-transfer resistance in the catalytic process.

In addition, to assess the reliability of THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 for enzyme inhibitor screening, kinetic studies were performed using argatroban as a reference inhibitor. A series of different concentrations of argatroban were added to 0.1 mM of the substrate S-2238, and the inhibition rates were calculated. The inhibition curve is obtained by plotting the rate of inhibition against the logarithm of inhibitor concentration, resulting in an IC50 of 52.47 nM (as shown in Fig. 6b). According to Eq. 3, the Ki value is calculated to be 4.6 nM, aligning closely with value reported in the literature [40]. Moreover, the Z ‘factor is calculated to be 0.96 (n = 3), indicating the high stability and reliability of this screening method. These results confirm the utility of THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 in the evaluation and screening of THR inhibitors. Fig. 6c and Fig. S7 confirm that flavonoids have inhibitory effects on THR, aligning with findings documented in the literature [41–43]. This method proves to be an accurate and rapid approach for screening THR inhibitors. Moreover, the study verifies the inhibitory effects of three alkaloids in Leonurus japonicus Houtt on THR.

The magnetic ligand fishing technology offers high selectivity and straightforward operation, enabling the rapid screening of active compounds. Argatroban and warfarin were used as positive and negative samples, respectively, to assess the feasibility and specificity of the magnetic ligand fishing method utilizing THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 in identifying THR inhibitors. As depicted in Fig. 6d，the positive sample peak appears only in the eluent, while no peaks corresponding to the negative sample are observed in either the control sample or the eluent. The introduction of the eluent denatures the immobilized THR, and argatroban, which specifically binds to THR, is subsequently eluted. This demonstrates that THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 can effectively screen THR inhibitors based on specific affinity interactions of drug with THR.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence biosensing and screening studies of enzyme inhibitors
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based nanomaterials are widely used in biomedical applications [44,45]. Among them, CDs stand out for their excellent fluorescence properties, offering new avenues for drug recognition and screening. Firstly, the fluorescence properties of CDs, THR@CDs and THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 were comparatively investigated. As shown in Fig. S8, both THR@CDs and THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 display excitation-dependent emission fluorescence behaviors similar to CDs, with the strongest fluorescence emission at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm. Moreover, as seen in Fig. 7a, the position of the fluorescence emission peak of THR@CDs is significantly blue-shifted compared to that of CDs, with a wavelength change (Δλ) of 5 nm. This shift originates from the coupling between the carboxyl groups of CDs and the amino groups of THR, resulting in weakened dipole-dipole interactions and a shorten Stokes shift. This alteration not only causes a blue shift in the emission peaks but also attenuates the intensity of the fluorescence emission peaks. Furthermore, the interaction between THR@CDs and ZIF-8 leads to a poor electron density distribution in THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8, further diminishing the fluorescence intensity.

To evaluate the sensitivity of the THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 biosensor in detecting THR inhibitors, the effect of varying concentrations of argatroban (0.05–2 μM) on the fluorescence intensity of THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 was investigated. As shown in Fig. 7b, with the increase of argatroban concentration, the fluorescence intensity of THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 gradually increase and the fluorescence emission wavelength significantly blue-shifts. This phenomenon is attributed to the interaction between THR and CDs/ZIF-8, which typically results in fluorescence quenching. When argatroban, which has a higher affinity for THR, binds to the enzyme, this quenching effect is weakened. As the concentration of argatroban increases, the specific interaction between THR and argatroban becomes more pronounced, enhancing the fluorescence intensity of THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8. Furthermore, a good linear relationship between F/F0 and the concentration of argatroban is observed. The limit of detection (LOD) of THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 for argatroban is 12.5 nM, which is comparable to previously reported values for fluorescence detection of argatroban [46]. Additionally, changes in the fluorescence spectra of CDs and THR@CDs in the presence of different concentrations of argatroban were also explored (Fig. S9). Different concentrations of argatroban has little effect on the fluorescence spectra of CDs. In contrast, the fluorescence intensity of THR@CDs enhances and the fluorescence emission wavelength blue-shifts as the concentration of argatroban increases. This is primarily due to the interaction between argatroban and THR. The LOD and limit of quantification (LOQ) of THR@CDs for argatroban is 25 nM and 0.1 μM, respectively. The lower LOD and LOQ values of THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 are attributed to the adsorption properties of the mesoporous MOFs and the encapsulation protection of THR@CDs, which enhance detection sensitivity. Thus, the THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 fluorescent biosensor is highly suitable for the sensitive detection of argatroban.

The selectivity of the THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 biosensor for fluorescence detection of inhibitors was explored using warfarin, adenosine, heparin, benzamidine and argatroban as potential inhibitors. As depicted in Fig. 7c, warfarin, adenosine, and heparin, which are not direct inhibitors of THR, do not elicit significant fluorescence enhancement. However, both benzamidine and argatroban, which bind strongly to THR, significantly enhance the fluorescence intensity of THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8. Notably, the enhancement observed with argatroban is greater than that with benzamidine, aligning with the inhibition trends of THR enzyme activity reported in the literature (benzamidine, Ki = 220 μM [47], argatroban, Ki = 39 nM [48]). It is evident that the THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8 biosensor has considerable potential for screening direct THR inhibitors.

In this study, a universal preparation method for novel enzyme-MOFs composite was developed. CDs prepared with DESs, which are rich in carboxyl groups, were successfully employed for the covalent modification of enzymes (THR@CDs). THR@CDs introduce defects into the MOFs through competitive coordination, resulting in the formation of mesoporous structures. The obtained enzyme-MOFs composite, THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8, exhibits higher affinity, enzyme activity and stability compared to THR/Fe3O4@ZIF-8. The modification of enzymes with CDs facilitates the in situ encapsulation process and accelerates the formation of enzyme-MOFs composite. THR@CDs/Fe3O4@ZIF-8, with high enzyme activity, affinity and strong fluorescence properties, enables sensitive recognition and screening of inhibitors through activity screening method, affinity screening method and fluorescent biosensor method. This simple and versatile synthesis strategy not only opens new pathways for the development of advanced enzyme-MOFs composite but also establishes a novel platform for drug recognition and screening based on MOFs-immobilized enzymes.

Min Wang: Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Yanjie Chen: Investigation, Data curation. Hongli Luo: Writing – original draft. Mingyue Wang: Software. Lingling Li: Validation. Zhining Xia: Supervision. Yong Xu: Funding acquisition. Yilan Huang: Writing – review & editing.
